critic_raw:
The churn prediction with sentiment features experiment has a specific quantitative claim — 15% accuracy improvement — that is not operationally defined, plus several structural problems.

The most critical problem is the headline claim: 'at least 15% improvement in churn prediction accuracy.' Accuracy is not the primary metric — AUROC is. A 15% improvement in what exactly? If it is 15% improvement in AUROC (from, say, 0.70 to 0.805), that is a very large effect. If it is a 15% relative improvement in accuracy (from 0.80 to 0.92), that is also extremely large. If it is 15% improvement in absolute accuracy (from 0.70 to 0.85 in a balanced setting), that is also large. The hypothesis states an exact quantitative threshold that is not operationally defined in the evaluation section — the experiment could be designed to technically confirm the hypothesis under one interpretation and refute it under another without changing a single model.

Second, churn data over three years with monthly labels has strong temporal structure. Users who churn in month 3 of a subscription are behaviorally different from users who churn in month 36. Sentiment from reviews submitted early in a subscription reflects a different engagement phase than late-subscription sentiment. A stratified random split ignores this temporal structure and will likely attribute temporal behavioral changes to sentiment signal, inflating the apparent importance of sentiment features.

Third, TF-IDF-encoded reviews as features require a vocabulary built from training documents. If the vocabulary or IDF weights are computed on the full dataset (including validation and test reviews) before splitting, this constitutes feature leakage from future text. The design states preprocessing is fit on training data, but TF-IDF corpus construction is often silently performed on the full dataset in common implementations (e.g., sklearn's default). This silent misconfiguration produces plausible-looking results that would collapse on a properly isolated test set.

Fourth, the baseline is logistic regression using only transactional data while the experimental model is gradient boosting using both transactional and sentiment features. This is a two-variable change: model architecture and feature set simultaneously. If gradient boosting wins, the experiment cannot determine whether the win came from sentiment features or from the gradient boosting architecture. A proper test requires gradient boosting on transactional features only as an additional baseline.
defender_raw:
The sentiment analysis feature augmentation hypothesis is mechanistically motivated: customer feedback text contains expressed dissatisfaction, frustration, or unmet expectations that precede churn in ways that transactional data alone (billing patterns, usage frequency) may not capture until it is too late. A user who writes a negative review but continues using the service for another month provides a churn signal in their text weeks before the transactional signal appears. Adding sentiment features tests whether this leading indicator adds predictive power over transactional trailing indicators.

The 15% improvement threshold in the hypothesis is a meaningful quantitative commitment. It prevents the experiment from declaring success on marginal or noise-level improvements and sets a bar that justifies the operational complexity of adding an NLP sentiment pipeline to a production churn prediction system. This kind of quantified hypothesis is a design strength.

Gradient boosting as the primary model is well-suited to the mixed-data structure: it handles both numerical transactional features and sentiment scores (continuous polarity, TF-IDF-derived features) without requiring architectural changes, and it is robust to the feature scale differences between these two data types. The logistic regression baseline on transactional-only data is a realistic representation of what simpler production systems use. The comparison tests both model type and feature augmentation simultaneously, which conflates two factors — but this is defensible if the goal is a practical deployment comparison rather than a pure ablation.

AUROC as primary metric is correct for a churn ranking application. Subscription services prioritize retaining the highest-risk users — this requires ranking ability, not a fixed threshold. The design acknowledges AUROC's threshold-independence as specifically suited to this operational use case.

The design honestly acknowledges that the stratified random split "could be contested" given temporal patterns in user interactions. This epistemic honesty is a design strength: the design is not pretending the temporal concern doesn't exist, it is making an explicit modeling assumption (weak autocorrelation) and committing to empirical validation. The confound controls (price changes, user behavior shifts as covariates, stratified subset analysis) partially operationalize this validation commitment.
all_issues_raised: The '15% improvement' claim is not operationally defined — improvement in AUROC, absolute accuracy, or relative accuracy produces very different thresholds; the hypothesis could be confirmed or refuted under different interpretations of the same number.

Monthly churn labels over three years have strong temporal structure; stratified random split conflates temporal behavioral change with sentiment signal, inflating apparent sentiment feature importance.
TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF weights are silently computed on the full corpus in many standard implementations — if this occurs before splitting, it constitutes feature leakage from future review text.

Baseline uses logistic regression + transactional data while the experimental model uses gradient boosting + transactional + sentiment — the two-variable change (architecture and features) prevents attributing any improvement to sentiment specifically.
all_issues_adjudicated: The '15% improvement' claim is not operationally defined — improvement in AUROC, absolute accuracy, or relative accuracy produces very different thresholds; the hypothesis could be confirmed or refuted under different interpretations of the same number.
TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF weights are silently computed on the full corpus in many standard implementations — if this occurs before splitting, it constitutes feature leakage from future review text.

Baseline uses logistic regression + transactional data while the experimental model uses gradient boosting + transactional + sentiment — the two-variable change (architecture and features) prevents attributing any improvement to sentiment specifically.
